CURRENT AFFAIRS, o

The “ truly Catholic”’ Freeman has failed to climb

" a Pastoral Kopje; so has the Independent. Our
readers will remember the war that we waged on the

3 _dirty imported stage in Dublin. When we came on the

scene over five years ago we discovered the Freeman, the

Independent, and the Irish Times puffing an indecent
usic-hall performance at the since happily defunct
yric. That was our first shot at the dirty theatre.

~ The Freeman and Independent, however, advertised
‘the dirty plays that have visited Dublin.

Notwith-
ing our exposures they advertised the dirty Saplo
play about a harlot. Well some people—who
probably wiser now—determined to import this

ing into Limerick. His Lordship the Most Rev. Dr.

_promptly came to the rescue, and a forcible
oral letter from him was read in all the churches of

—

Limerick on Sunday last. The Freeman, whose re-
venue has more than once been added to by money re-
ceived for advertising this filthy piece, evidently had
not the pluck to refuse insertion of the text of Dr.
O’Dwyer’s pastoral in its news columns; but the “truly
Catholic ** organ jibbed at climbing the Pastoral Kopje.
There is not an editorial whisper about the matter in
its editorial columns.

His lordship, Most Rev. Dr. O’Dwyer, in the course
of his letter on immoral plays, said:—“Above all, it is
shocking to every sense of propriety to hear of young
girls being prescnt at such plays. - Self-respect, the
very externals of modesty, which any girl who is not
utterly abandoned will maintain, should make it im-
possible for them to look on at scenes which it would
be regarded as an insult for any man to describe to
them in words.”” Tt is also shocking to every sense of
propriety that the Freeman and the Independent have
repeatedly advertised such plays. Both these papers
pretend to be Irish, and yet as his lordship remarks:—
“ Most, if not all, of this abominable stuff comes from
England, and deals with phases of English life to
which, thank God, the Irish people are strangers.
Surely we have strength enough to stand by our own
principles and to maintain our own ethical standards,
and not be dragged into the mire by any other people.’’
These are the plays that the Freeman and Independent
have persisted in advertising whenever they have been
dumped here. With the experience of “ Sapho ** before
them we do not suppose “Zaza’’ and “Kitty Grey
will be in any hurry to dump themselves in Limerick.

The heading “ Rowdy Students’’ caught our eye in
one of the pages of a British contemporary. The Earl
of Meath would read the news underneath with keen
interest.  Or, perhaps, he wouldn’t, for the heading
refers to ructions at Glasgow University during the re-
cent election of Rector. We read in this British con-
temporary :—*“ From early morning the leading city
thoroughfares were perambulated by male and female
undergrads in brakes, lorries, cabs, and motor-cars,
from which they pelted each other and passing pedes-
trians with peasemeal, soot, and eggs. Outside the en-
trance to the polling-station, in Bute Hall of the Uni-
versity, there was a great gathering of students in all
sorts of dress, many turning out in football uniform.
A continual fusilade of peasemeal and other missiles was
maintained, with the result that voters presented the
appearance of quay-lumpers, who had been discharging
mealy cargoes.”” ~ What “ill-conditioned’’ male and
female undergraduates they must have in Glasgow.

We are further told that  fierce fighting raged round
the entrance.”” And we read :—“ During the progress
of the fighting which took place a medical Freshman,
named Robertson, had his leg broken, and another sus-
tained a fractured arm. Both were conveyed to Weston
Infirmary close by. In the melee many had their
clothes torn from their backs, and several students
were carried out of the crush in a fainting condition,
whilst dozens could be seen limping about.”” The Earl
of Meath, with his Fifth Battalion of Royal Dublin
Fusiliers, should have been at Glasgow on that occasion.

The programme of the recent concert in Dublin in
aid of the O’Donovan Rossa Testimonial Fund was not
what could be called a tee-total programme. Mr. Bung
spread himself all over it. On the first page three
Bungs made their bow; on the second page we find
four Bungs, one of them exhorting as follows:— Let
all Gaels drink succcss to O’Donovan Rossa, and the
principles for which he fought and suffered at"=——.""
There’s Bung, the green patriot, for you! On page

three we have five Bungs; in the centre pages, four and
five, there are four Bungs, next page there are four, one
of» whom broke out into rhyme : g

“If you ever want good malt to buy,
Or a creamy pint to try,
Call at —.”

There are four Bungs on next page, and five on the last.
In all, at least, twenty-nine Bungs figure on this concert
programme, and yet Ireland is not free!

A prominent firm of Dublin manufacturers write
us :— As you take considerable interest in the G.S. and
‘W. Railway Co., perhaps the following would be in-
teresting to yourself and your readers:—One of- our
agents endeavoured,to open a trade in the Bandon dis-
trict. We made some small deliveries to Bandon and
Skibbereen, but the freight charged by the Railway
Cos. was so excessive as to put further business quite
out of the question. Particulars are as follows:—

To Skibbereen—1 case freight on gross, 2s. 7d. per

cwt. =52s. per ton gross; or=G60s.
per ton nett.
To Bandon—I case 2s. per cwt. —40s. per ton gross; or
=4bs. per ton nett.”’

“This is merely station to station, and includes no
charges at either Dublin or Skibbereen. For your in-
formation, we are aware that 10s. per ton is a very
general rate from Chicago to Liverpool. In justice to
the G. S. and W., we may say that the Cork and Ban-
don Railway is responsible for portion of these excessive
charges; but between them their rates make business
impossible. We may mention that the Railway Co.
quote for lots from 4 cwt. to 20 cwt.—

To Skibbereen SHa DS 1'd

To Bandon 30s. 10d.
But needless to say, trade is very seldom opened with
such large orders.”” No wonder the traffic receipts of the
Great Sourface Railway for 17 weeks working are down
£15,546.

L. Childs, Kilruddery, Bray, Co. Wicklow, adver-
tised a “ want’’ in the “Zrish’’ TWmes of November
3rd, at which we smiled. Poor man; those who expect
uothing will not be disappointed, but we fear he will
be wanting his “want”” for many a long day. His
“want *’ is as follows : —“ Wanted, educated Protestant,
about 16 years of age, for the gardening profession—
Bothy, L. Childs, Kilruddery, Bray, Co. Wicklow.”
The. whole country ought to give L. Childs what help
it can in his bold exploring campaign; and the woods
and marches throughout the country ought to be well
beaten in the hope of starting an “ educated Protestant,
about 16 years of age.” But we fear L. Childs might
as well be advertising for a white blackbird.

- The undergraduates of the Royal University who by
a master . stroke of boyish tactics, which Ireland ap-
plauds, prevented the organ playing “ Damn the Idola-
tors,”” on the last conferring day, have trod heavily on
the corns of the Bigott 7%mes of London; it will be
remembered that they also twisted the tail of the Orange
News Letter. The British Forger is almost as amusing
as was the News Letter from Belfast, where the noto-
riously tolerant > Corporation comes from. Says the
Fornger:—*: . . a gang of young men, almost
exclusively consisting, we are informed, of students from
the Roman Catholic College in Stephen’s Green and
from the Cecilia Street School of Medicine, also Roman
Catholic, assembled in the hall, evidently to defeat the
execution of the promise in the programme that the
proceedings would terminate with a performance-of the
National Anthem on the great organ.”

Again the angelic Forger and friond to the late Mr.
Pigott, adds to the gaiety of Irish readers by remark-
ing:—“TIt is fair to assume that the Senate of the
University, which includes men of the highest position

unblemished loyalty, will do their
promptly at this grave crisis.”” That

to every loyal and self-respecting man among those
responsible for its rule.”

The Times remarks that one of the leading members
of this Dublin Castle Board, called by the name of a
Senate, has announced that he will resign if vigorous
anti-Irish action is not taken. We are glad to hear it.
We sincerely hope that all the unblemished loyz;l.)s,s
will resign not only from this Castle Board but from
every Castle Board.

The fact that Mr. Matthews, a draper in the village
of Dunleer, a small place with a population of 459, won
the prize offered to the drvapers of Louth and Meath,
who had bought most Irish manufactured goods within
a certain period, showed like a flash what the shop-
keepers, even the small shop-keepers, could do for Irish
industry if they put their hearts into the work. A cor-
respondent of ours who knows the _country well, or
ought to know it, recently remarked in the course of a
letter to us:— The small shops in the country and in
villages and small towns have the country people at
their mercy—immense influence for good or bagl;and
alas! these shops are absolutely indifferent to tl}ell' duty
of supporting Irish manufacture.”” We are indeed a
sort of inanimate, as well as a green, people. A shop
woman said recently to an enquirer, “ What can I do?
T must stock what they ask for.”” And this woman’s
windows were filled up with foreign goods! No wonder
the people asked for them; why could she not have the
gumption to put Irish goods in the window, and per-
haps the people would get into the habit of asking for
them. The woman of the shop fills her windows with
foreign goods because the people ask for them; the
people ask for foreign goods, probably to a large extent,
because the woman fills her windows with them. The
woman says, “ What can I do, I must stock what they
ask for,”” The green and rather inanimate customers no
doubt would say, “ What can we do, the shop only
stocks foreign goods, we can always get them?”

A new industry for the manufacture of vestments,
hosiery, dolls, etc., has recently been started at Bess-
brook, Co. Armagh, and is now in full working order.
The industry is in connection with the local Convent of
Mercy, which some economists would probably describe
as a branch of “the unproductive classes.”’

A trader in Virginia, Co. Cavan, sent to a Dublin
firm for a price list on the 20th October. Five days after,
the firm sent a postcard as follows:—‘In reply to
yours requesting price list kindly send us proof that
you are in the trade, together with 3d. to cover cost of
postage.” Our Virginian correspondent says—Is it
any wonder we have to go abroad sometimes for goods

~when we get replies like this. It was time enough for

him to make enquiries when he received my order.””

Dr. M‘Walter is tackling the consumption problem
with characteristic vigour. His pamphlet, “ The Con-
sumption Problem in Dublin—and How to Deal With
It,’’-is-one that deserves a wide circulation. The great
white plague as consumption has been appropriately, if
grimly, named, exacts from Dublin a heavy yearly tri-
bute in human lives. Last year the number of deaths
for which it was responsible was 1,678, and it is esti-
mated that.from 5,000 to 6,000 personsin various stages

of the disease are in the city and suburbs to-day. As"

to the unhappy part played by the tenement evil in this
state of affairs, Dr. M‘Walter tells us much. After quot-
ing from Professor Roche regarding one-room tenements.

'f

GREZFT

~_ NoveMsER 11, 1905.

* he writes as follows—"“ It appears, therefore, that close
upon 100,000 in Dublin live in one-room tenements ; that
5,000 or 6,000 persons, mostly of this class, have con-
sumption, and that over 1,600 persons die of this dis-
ease in Dublin every year. There’s the dread situation.
‘What are we doing to combat the disease?’’ Dr. M‘Wal-
ter strongly comments on the present hospital resources
for.Dublin consumptives, and urges the need of a special
sanitorium.  That would, of course, cost money, but
improvements nowadays are seldom got without, and we
would recommend those people who lay over-stress on
financial obstacles to ponder over one statement of Dr.
M‘\Vglter:—“ At the present day, then, in the City of
Dubl{n, when a poor man gets consumption, there is
practically no place as yet where he has a fair chance of
recovery. His doom is sealed, and he is simply fated to
be one unit in the Dublin weekly tribute of thirty deaths
from the disease.””

Messrs. McMenamin Bros., the enterprising blacking
manufacturers of Rathfarnham, have recently purchased
a fine covered car for exhibition purposes. The car
which was designed, built and painted by Messrs.
Mahon, Coach Builders, Gloucester Street, is a hand-
some specimen of Irish manufacture. A figure of a
leprecz_um appears on each side, and from whatever point
one views it striking announcements relating to the
articles manufactured by the firm catch the eye. When
going its rounds in Dublin, with a driver dressed in
special uniform of Irish material, we have no doubt it
will attract much attention. Messrs. McMenamin have
given their Irish-made polishes the title of Leprecaun.
We submitted some samples of Leprecaun polish to
expert opinion in the polishing line, and we were pleased
to hear a most favourable account of their merits.

Mr. Stephen Gwynn, in the course of his remarks at
Rathmines, as reported in the Fréeeman, said: —‘ With
the study of Irish the Catholic clergy naturally had
sympathy.  They might look for sympathy to the
Church of Ireland, but he was afraid they would not
get it. They would not get it from the Protestant
clergy of the North, who considered themselves more
Scotch than Irish.”

We have received the English version of  Cathair
Conroi,” and other tales from the pen of “ Deic
Feap”” (Mr. J. J. Doyle, of Derry). These tales were
originally composed by Mr. Doyle for the Oireachtas of
1902, at which they won the prize for children’s stories.
These tales in théir Irish dress are on the syllabus of
the Preparatory and Junior Grades in the Inter-
mediate, and have had a_successful sale. The pre-
sent English translation will be welcomed by those who
are not sufficiently forward in TIrish to appreciate
them in that form without a key. The new publication
is issued by Messrs. Sealy, Bryers and Walker, Dublin,
and may be had for 6d.

In the course of a brief first notice of the “ Philo-
sophy of Irish Ireland,’”’ which it calls a “thoughtful
and thought-provoking book,”” our Australian contem-
porary, the Southern Cross of Adelaide, says:—“We
hope to deal more fully with this book, almost every
page of which tempts quotation, when space and leisure
permit. In the meantime we can cordially recommend
it to all those who wish to study the ideas which have
made the LEADER perhaps the greatest literary ‘force
in Ireland since the Nation. The volume, which con-

5 ‘ta.ins 114 pages, is well worth the 1s. asked for it.”’

A second course of lectures in “ Astronomy for the
People,’” was opened by Rev. E. A. Selley, 0.0.A., in
the poﬂee Palace, Townsend .Street, on last Monday
evening. Father Selley chose for his initial lecture “The

lead of the Family,” this being his humorous de-
- scription of the sun, and his able treatment kept a large

udience intensely interested from start to finish, The

THE LEADER. - 119

title of the course, “ Astronomy for the People,” was
well selected, and Father Selley has certainly the faculty
for making this scientific subject understandable to the
man in the hall. The lecture was suitably illustrated
by lantern slides, and the second one in the course of
six will be delivered on the first Monday in December.
Reoserved tickets for the six lectures may be obtained at
the Coffec Palace at 2s., and tickets for family at 5s.
The prices for admission to a night lecture are 6d. 3d.
and 2d.

We trust we will cause no alarm in peaceful Rath-
mines by announcing that that local centie of activity,
the Catholic Association, has begun winter operations.
The first of the series of social evenings was held in
the rooms 78a Rathmines Road, on Hallow Eve, and was
most_enjoyable and successful.  In addition to this
popular feature the attractions of the Association in-
clude a dancing-class, debating society, etc. The next
social evening will take place on Tuesday, 14th inst.

One would think that Claremorris, if not a centre of
enlightened nationality, would at least be a hot bed of
the Green variety. A District Inspector of Police by
the name of Carbery, is being removed—we assume on
promotion—to Drogheda. Surely this “ minion ** would
be left to go at least in peace from Claremorris in the
County of Mayo, God help us! Nothing of the kind.
The wealthy and generous people of Claremorris will
insist upon giving this pillar of British Government in
Ireland a testimonial. It is thought,”’ says the inevi-
table circular, “ that the people of Claremorris and the
neighbourhood may wish to present Mr. Carbery, D.I.,
with some token of their esteem on the occasion of his
removal to Drogheda. FHis courtesy and ability in the
discharge of his duties have been recognised by all, and
he has taken a prominent part in our social life by the
great services which he has rendered on the Committee
of the Claremorris Horse and Agricultural Show, which
have contributed to achieve the great success which has
crowned that undertaking.”

The circular is signed by Lord Oranmore, John P.
Egan, A. J. Blake, Colonel J. A. Lambert, T. P.
Donnellan, Garrett Nally, and the Hon. Sec. is one J.
Crean. Lord Oranmore, it will be remembered, is the
man Geoffrey Henry Browne Browne-Guthrie, who some
time ago made a genius of himself in the British House
of Lords by suggesting that “ Perhaps it might be made
compulsory that the National Anthem (he meant the
‘Damn the Nationalists’ one) should be taught and
sung in every National School.”

T. P. Donnellan, one of those who wants to raise the
wind for this policeman is a “ Nationalist’’ District
Councillor—God help us; Garrett Nally is a  tinint
farmer—God help us! A. J. Blake is an aristocrat in
these quarters for he is Clerk to Petty Sessions at
Ballyhaunis, and the Secretary J. Crean is another
blue-blooded individual, for he is Clerk to Petty Ses-
sions at Claremorris. We are sure it makes them feel
“tony ’’ to have their names along with that of Loxd
Oranmore to a circular that strives to stimulate the
sending round the hat for a police “ inspectaw.” TLam-
bert is a militia Colonel. Now what has this policeman
done to deserve anything at the hands of the people
of Claremorris? Must people be always cringing and
fawning on ‘ minions”’ of the British Law in Ireland
when they are sent from one place to another? Surely’
even the “ tony *’ patriots of Claremorris—God help us!
ought to have more self-respect.

The  Irish’’ T'imes treats the demon_stra,bions that
took place ab the departure from Dublin of Douglas
Hyde, en route for America, to the eloquence of silence.
What a perfect “ Irish *’ newspaper, to be sure! The
demonstration, by all accounts, was a big affair; bub
demonstratidns are rather discounted forms of expres-

sion in Ireland; the eloquent silence of the “Zrish’’
T'smes, however, underlines the significance of this par-
ticular demonstration.  Our sprightly contemporary
had not even the heart to attempt to make an elephan-
tine joke about it. The waters are creeping up steadily,
if slowly, on poor West Britain embedded in the
slob-lands of Ireland.

We have before us a page from an Armagh book-
seller’s list on which there is a list of books under the
heading, “Irish Language.”’ It is not without reason
that the heading is there, anyway. Could any Irish
scholar tell us what these books, taken from the list,
are:—“ An Ceur Leaban Sacoilge,”” “Troaet an Scro-
caine,” “ Laoi Oipin ap Tip Nanoy,”” “ Certpe Sycuta,’’
“Prop Taedesluc,”’ “ Fopup Peapu?’ These are a few
specimens from this brilliant Irish department of this
book list. We understand that this list is published by
Mr. W. J. Greer, Armagh. Is this man a pioneer in the
work of bringing Irish spelling up to date? Are there
no book-sellers in Armagh who can place a list of Irish
books in a catalogue without making this sort of an ex-
hibition? Is there no Irish class in Armagh? and if
there is; why not bring some pressure to bear upon the
compilers of this ludicrous travesty of the titles of Irish
books? Who is Mr. W. J. Greer? Is he a Nationalist
and an Irish Irelander?

‘We hear that many persons are working hard in the
cause of temperance in Newry, and particularly in view
of this fact the programme of a concert held on Monday
last in connection with the Christian Schools, Newry,
is very unsatisfactory, to say the least. There are no less
than twenty-six Bung advertisements scattered through-
out the programme of this “ highly attractive concert.”
Bung we know is a powerful interest, and many people
are afraid to tread on his toe; but at least one would
expect the programme of a concert in connection with
the Christian Schools, Newry, would have given an
example in this respect. One of the Bung’s who ad-
vertises in this programme, not unappropriately carries
on the undertaking business as well as the drink enter-
prise! If Bung was well on the programme, the name
of a song in Irish was conspicuous by its absence. We
find that a performer by the name of Doctor Dick was
down for a “ humourous sketch,’”’ which the programme
tells us was an “ imitation of Mr. Horace Wheatley.”
When will Ireland strike against imitation? One of
the items was an Irish dance, but perhaps by way of
showing that the promoters were no narrow-minded
little Irelanders, even in the dancing way, we find also
a Spanish, a French, and an Italian dance on the pro-
gramme.

CORRESPONDENCE.

FRANCE AND IRELAND.
16 Rue Lauriston,
Paris, October 23rd, 1905.

DEAR SiR,—I am happy to inform you that I de-
livered a lecture at the Geographical Society at Lille
(Nord) on last Thursday, the 19th inst., about “ Ireland
tiB% the 20th Century.”” The audience was more than

0.

After a historical exposure of the agrarian and social
questions in Ireland, I told the actual situation of the
country, and gave an account of the Celtic (intellectual,
Gaelic, economical and political) revival:

As a conclusion, I told that we must never forget that
France is Celtic as well as Ireland, and that we were, we

THE LEADER.

NoveMBER 11, 1905.

are, and we will always be the best, and most sincere
friends of Erin. Ireland and the Irish Revival meb
loud applauses. .
Projections with the magic lantern took place during
that lecture, a part of them being towns, zwb'beys, and
landscapes, and the other part being the shocking scenes
of an eviction, and views of the congested districts.
I am happy to send you this account of my lecture
and to tell you how valuable to me was, for the pre-
paration of it, the weekly reading of your so interesting
Leaper. I intend to deliver this winter other lectures
about Ireland.—Please believe me, Dear Sir, faithfully
yours, YANN MORVRAN GOBLET.

ENGLAND’S FAITHFUL SENATE.

T will be remembered that a few cads of the Soupers’
University in College Green, flung mud at a Lord
Mayor of Dublin some years ago.  There was no
specially summoned meeting of the Governors of that
discredited school associated with the odour of not over-
fresh herrings, and no Chancellor condemned the
Trinity mud-throwing cads in a speech handed to the
Press for publication. But then the mud-throwing
Trinity cads were “loyalists,” and no doubt “saved’’
in the bargain.

One Reginald Brabazon, better known in “ Imperial’’
circles as the Earl of Meath, is Chancellor of the par-
ticular Dublin Castle Board that goes by the name of
the Senate of the Royal University of Ireland. This
gentleman does not appear to be a graduate of any
‘University ; he has not even had “ education’ thrust
upon him in the shape of an honorary degree. If he
does not possess even an honorary degree he is at
least an honorary Thomas Atkins, for Reginald Bra-
bazon is a honorary Colonel of the fifth Battalion Royal
Dublin Fusiliers. This “ honorary ’’ son of Mars in the
course of a violent, if decidedly foolish, harangue,
said :—*“ The outrage of October 27th ought in no way
to be compared to the noisy, silly, but often good-
humoured interruptions which are too frequently cus-
tomary, not only in this University, but elsewhere.’’
From this it would seem that with the Earl’s consent,
we may assume that all that might be said against the
action of the students on the ground of noisy and
rowdy conduct may be eliminated. Boyish rowdyism on
the part of University undergraduates is condoned,
rightly or wrongly, on certain occasions; even the Chan-
cellor of this Dublin Castle Board expressly admitted
that that sort of thing goes on at other Universities;
indeed, our objection to the ordinary and customary
rowdyism of Royal University undergraduates is that
it is rather an imitation of what goes on at the Souper
University and other places. The point is clear that
it was not the rowdyism but the nationality of the
undergraduates clever escapade that put the English
Z'imes and smaller fry nearer home on the boil, and
that heated the “honorary '’ martial blood of Reginald
Brabazon, one of England’s Faithful Guardians in .this
country. It is purely and simply a National issue, and
as a National issue we take our stand at once with the
ingenious and spirited students who succeeded in pre-
venting the playing of the party tune of the Sourfaces,
“Damn the Nationalists,” a tune which is disguised
in this country under the misleading title of “ God Save
the King.”” The quarrel of this country is.not with

THE ANTI-ERLISTING CRUSADE.

I HAVE read Miss Milligan’s reply to my article on

the anti-enlisting crusade, and I must say that,
though she is courteous, she is not convincing, nor is
she strictly ingenuous. I did not say that Irishmen
are ‘“ bound to enlist ’ and that it is *“ hopeless to try
to prevent them.” T simply spoke of the present
sporadic anti-enlisting efforts as ‘‘ this impracticable
crusade,”’ and followed by saying: ‘‘ Enlistment must
be fought upon other lines altogether.” Is this the
same thing as saying that it must not be fought at all?
Because I want practicable lines, must I not deprecate
impracticable ones? =~ What on earth has a non-
political language movement to do with enlistment, and
why must some of the workers in the movement mix
the two things up together?

In my article of October 28th I set down tempera-
mental considerations as accounting for the majority
of enlistments, yet Miss Milligan seems to fancy that I
regard lack of employment as the main cause. Accord-
ingly she tells us that work can be had at any hiring- -
fair in the country towns. What good is that to men
who absolutely won’t take to that sort of life? There
are plenty such men both in England and Ireland
Does the slum-born youth make a good farm-hand?
He knows nothing about the work, and would not, like
that line of life at all. Large numbers of those who
enlist, do so in the greater cities. How can the man
who enlists at Linenhall Street engage himself at a
hiring-fair in Munster? =

It is not quite enough to provide work;
provide some kind of work tllx)ab men are f’iby?; !1;\;53
wxll. take to. The town-bred man will not ba.i(e to.
aguculbura} labour ; hence the need of industrial de-
velopments'to use up the labour of the available towns-
men and girls in factories and workshops.  Still, no
matter what work you provide, there are men who, in
their younger days at all events, will not take to. it
b!.xt. will enlist or run away to sea. But they are un:
wise? they are unpatriotic? Pooh! A large part of
mankind everywhere is unwise and unpatriotic—even:
of those who don’t enlist, and don’t g0 to sea.

NoveMBER 11. 1905.

I would point out to Miss Milligan that I never said
‘that it was ““ not an infrequent thing ”’ for soldiers to
commit suicide at the end of a long service, through
-depression of spirits at having to relinquish the army.
I simply said: ‘“ Men have been known to commit
suicide,’”” etc.—making no use of the word ‘infre-
-quent.”” The preachy sentence with which this un-
fair paraphrase is followed up simply shows how much
the spirit of propagandism warps the judgment of the
propagandist. Because a few time-expired men com-
mit felo-de-se, the soldier’s vocation is one that leads to
*“such a tragic consummation *’ that young men should
be warned against it! As wise were’it to suggest that
because a few wretches commit forgery, no one should
be taught writing, or because hundreds of people are
killed every year in railway accidents, nobody should
travel by rail. Miss Milligan cites dreadful things
-k done by soldiers—brandishing of swords, and such like
—as evidence of the dreadful effect of camp-life. I
could cite much worse things done by men who never
were in the army, thereby proving (by a parity of rea-
g soning) the much greater moral danger of remaining
[ 7 a civilian! Picked-out cases do not prove everything;

‘that is where the force and value of legitimate statis-
tics come in. Miss Milligan seems to think that garri-
son towns are hot-beds of intemperance. Well, let us
compare a county where there are many gairison towns,
with one where there are fewer. I have before me the
statistics of persons convicted of drunkenness three
times, and upwards, in the various counties of Ireland
in 1899—I have not selected that year specially
I find that in Tipperary, where there are many mi
centres, the total of such convictions was 155
Kerry, where there are fewer military centres, it was
308. In county Kildare, which contains two camps
and a cavalry station, the number was 65; in county
Clare it was 118. In Cork City, where there is a large
military body, it was 152; in county Tyrone (not a
great military centre, I think), it was 205. Lven in
* Dublin City, where the population is so large, and
where there are so many soldiers, the total falls 59
short of Kerry’s record. There may be “riot, drunken-
ness and disorder ’’ in garrison towns, as Miss Milligan
tells us, but for all this we see that the counties with
some of the largest garrison towns show up much better
than some of the less soldier-ridden counties. I think
these facts speak for themselves.  Taking Thomas
Atkins all round, I think he is neither a drunkard nor
a blackguard, the tale of the army pensioner who killed
his whole family, notwithstanding. Have not civilians
done the like? Deeming, for example, and Devereux ?
“What about ‘‘ affrays between farmers and soldiers’’ ?
Are there not five times as many affrays between civi-
lians themselves. = Miss Milligan will have it, ap-
parently, that Catholic theology counts it a deadly sin
“to enter the army; let her go and tell that to Bishop
-Brindle, D.S.0., till we hear what his lordship says.
i So far as I could ever see, soldiers are, on the whole,
B! rather a well-conducted body of men than otherwise.
Moreover, many a man who wore the red coat was a
better Irishman than some who were never guilty of
such turpitude.  There were many soldiers in the
“I.R.B., but the informers, if I mistake not, were all
-civilians. Again, how many people like Mr. Charles
Martin is a splendid Irishman like Sir William Butler
worth? How many Irish Catholic civilians of high
position would have had the grit to speak out about
. ‘Cromwell as Sir William Butler, a general of the Royal
Army, did? Few, indeed, I'll warrant! The plucky
~stand which Irish privates made for the right to wear
‘the shamrock on St. Patrick’s Day is a proof that being
in the army is not so destructive of patriotic feeling as
~ the anti-enlisters say. Indeed these crusaders overdo
bhe thing altogether, and simply put one out of con-
ceit with them. I allow, with the greatest willingness,
“that all the I.R.B. men were not make-believers; that
“there were many indeed among them who were really
‘tried and true.” Why will not the anti-enlisters
llow any virtue or merit to the Irish Tommy? Why,
ou could not compose any large organisation exclu-

sively of blackguards if you tried; no, nor even mainly
of such, for it would not hold together. The I.R.B.
assiduously sought for recruits in the army, but Athe
anti-enlister will not welcome any such wretches into
Irish Ireland, so much more ‘‘advanced’’ th'an the
Fenians is the anti-enlister! Stephens and his men
would allow red-coats into their organisation, but
your tolerant anti-enlister will not allow soldiers to
look on at Gaelic sports!  What a broad-minded
people, to be sure! I would rather have Sir William
Butler’s little finger than a score of such narrow-
headed cranks. The Irish Tommies who took punish-
ment for wearing the shamrock—and had their way,
in the end—were better fellows than the chaps who,
being caught posting anti-enlisting placards, allowed
their counsel to plead their youth among the other
convincing reasons why they should be allowed to defy
the authorities on the cheap! Certes, these dare-devils
(defended by counsel!) have all the patriotism of Ire-
land in their hands—and paste-pots—and if they don’t
save Ireland, what chance has God of doing it?

Miss ligan quite too sensitive in the opening
of her last paragraph, and appears to suppose I, in
some way, had her personally in view in my remarks. I
can assure her, without any mental reserves, that T had
not. Indeed the chief people I had in mind at the
time of writing my article were some anti-enlisters
down in Mayo. But my remarks in general were
directed against the movement in general. The case is
not to be met by these mere adventitious methods—
chiefly applied by chaps. These negative movements
never succeed ; mere ‘‘ anti ’-anything never does more
than a fraction of what it aims at—at best. Forma-
tive movements, education and industry: these are
what we want. Educate the people; employ them;
give them another and a better political atmosphere,
and enlisting will shrink to reasonable proportions.
But of course ‘‘ reasonable proportions’’ won’t satisfy
the anti-enlisters; they have ‘‘the pure milk of the
word >’ of patriotism, and consequently won’t have
anything short of perfection—which is an easy thing
to get, n this world! The author of T'he Philosophy
of Irish Ireland tells us that ‘‘ imperfect humanity, at
the best, will always go right in curves.”” But your
anti-enlister knows better, and he ‘‘ wants no blooming
curves.”” He stands for the ideal, sirs; the ideal de-
fended by counsel—and pleading its youth! “ Why
cannot, fools and men who may be half good and half
bad be borne with?’’ asks the author of the Philosophy
before quoted. =~ Why, only because our impatient
patriots want perfection, and want it right off. S

Miss Milligan refers in the end of her article to Kil-
clooney Wood. Well, T know something about that,
and may refer to it at some future date; for the pre-
sent T must close here. IMAAL.

"Affairs are moving fa~t in Russia. 
Not the least

Our reader~ will remember that an honorary

The 
occasion of this disloyal manifes,tation was the annual 
distribution of premiums or "commencement." At this 
function, Prince Midhe, the Governor, a noted sup-
porter of General Trepoff' s policy, presided. 
The 
earlier part of the proceedings was entirely orderly and 
the University authorities were thus led to believe that 
the recent relaxation of repressive measures had had its 
·effect and that the painful scenes of last autumn when 
the University was closed and handed over to the mili-
tary need not be repeated on this occasion. 
The peaceful appearance of affairs proved, however, 
to be entirely delusive, to be in part the prelude of a 
far graver manifestation of political disaffection than 
has hitherto taken place. 
The demonstrations, which 
have up to this been directed against the irresponsible 
government of the University, have now been diverted 
-into a new channel, and the growing sense of broken 
faith and disappointed . hopes on the part of the stu-

Profound distrust of all 
official assurances, especially in regard to University 
matters, is the common sentiment throughout Russia.

poignantly contradicted by the continuance of the old

must be speedily reversed. Official proclamations, no

1§ immunity of all persons who have taken part in the
recent political proceedings, and in particular of the

| arisen, and with this situation the irresponsible officials
in Poland and in the provinces will have to fall into
I line. A full amuesty, followed by a real change in the

dition of popular feeling manifested by the recent re-

(A government, in University education, are amongst the
elementary reforms that the Russian government has
still to concede, and we believe it to be the duty of the
Tsar to brush aside the interested counsels of the
bureaucracy in this matter, and to take a personal
initiative in fulfilling those promises which he himself
has made, or which have at least been uttered in his
name. Unless he adopts this course, which we believe
to be the only fit and honest one for a monarch who
